famous quote:
"You can't know, you can only believe - or not."
-- C.S. Lewis
Interview Text:
Thomas from Denmark:

I went to a church service whre there was a pastor from Copenhagen, Denmark who was in Atlanta for his sabbatical and the Grace Midtown pastor asked him to tell a story. He stood up and took the mic. In his rich Scandanavian accent in which "th" was always pronounced as "ff," He told us he felt he was to share something with us that his therapist had taught him. He encouraged us not to worry what other people think of us because we will never attain satisfaction, but only what God thinks of us, and God always thinks we're enough. Then he very dryly added, "But with a body like mine, it's hard not to feel like you're always enough."

He was a broad-shouldered, thick-wasted bald man with big, thick, black-framed glasses, wearing a white t-shirt and he delivered everything about as animated as a snowman. And he was very likable.

He told how he was invited to a dinner where they would talk about diversity. He had no idea what to expect but he showed up and found that the other dozen or so invitees were quite important in Denmark. Just to name a few, there was the youngest millionaire in Denmark, the founder of Denmark's version of Amazon and a member of the Denmark parliament.

The topic of discussion was "Is technology good or bad for mankind." Thomas felt grossly out of place amongst these intellectual giants. But at some point in the meeting he felt God say, "Tell them that mankind is broken." 

Thomas was suddenly terrified to tell this to such powerful, self-made people and so instead of speaking he got up and went to the bathroom. But after he got back and was distracting himself with the food, God told him again, "You must tell them that mankind is broken." 

And so when they asked him, he summoned his bravery and said, "I don't think there is a problem with technology. My belief system says that the problem lies with mankind. It is the heart of man that is broken."

At the end of the meeting they were each asked to say something they learned and four of the twelve people said, "I learned that it is the heart of mankind that is broken." 

That night he recieved an email from the parliament member saying if he needed any finances for his ministry he could ask him. A couple of the millionaires emailed him telling him how what he had taught them had changed the way they look at things, and even the facilitator of the group told him how much what he had said helped him and in fact asked if he could host the next diversity talk at his church. He suggested to Thomas that the topic of discussion should be the brokenness of mankind.

I thought, if he heard God's voice certain enough to say that to those men, he must really recognize when it is God's voice. So after the service I went up to him and asked. 
He thought for a moment and then said, "How do you say it in English. I'd say it is about . . . practice. There are a lot of voices in our heads and so you must practice and test them out to see which one is His. And after years of trying them out you learn to recognize which one is his."
Music Video Instructions: Imagine if God's love for us, and ours for him was like this.
